general: Wow, another console.log. Bold debugging strategy.
The docs called. They said you never visit.
Love how you skipped the error handling like it's optional.
That variable name really tells a story. A bad one.
have you tried turning it off and on again?
So courageous of you to write that without checking the types.
Ah yes, the classic 'it works on my machine' defense.
Your code and the requirements doc are in different timelines.
Impressive. You managed to use git wrong.
That TODO comment has been there since 2023, hasn't it.
The linter just sighed audibly.
Copy-paste is not an architecture pattern.
Your stack overflow answer is from 2014. Just saying.
Ah, the 'I'll refactor this later' comment. Sure you will.
That function is doing way too much for its own good.
Did you just hard-code that API key? No judgment. Okay, some judgment.
Your code review would like a word.
The compiler is doing its best with what you gave it.
That merge conflict resolution was... optimistic.
You're really testing the limits of 'any' as a type, huh.
Your code reads like you were high. You were, weren't you.
Ah, another npm install that'll bloat node_modules further.
That callback hell is architecturally... ambitious.
Your IDE must be really earning its linting badge today.
You wrote a 200-line function and not a single comment. Hero.
The type system called. It wants to press charges.
I see you're using ! to silence the compiler. That'll end well.
Your code has more workarounds than actual code.
That's a lot of // eslint-disable-next-line for one file.
The runtime called. It's filing for emancipation.
I've seen better code written by a caffeinated squirrel.
Your keyboard called. It wants a vacation.
That bug was you. It was always you.
Your code is like onion — every layer makes me cry.
You write code like someone paying by the semicolon.
Even the syntax highlighter is confused.
That's a big if statement. Emotional damage included.
Your function has more branches than a bank.
I'd explain why that's wrong but you'd just argue.
Your test coverage called in sick today.
That regex is a crime scene.
You really said 'it's fine' and committed, didn't you.
Your git history is a cry for help.
That's not a bug, that's your personality.
Your code reviews must be gripping. Short, but gripping.
The interpreter is judging you. We all are.
You deployed that on a Friday. Absolute madlad.
That variable has more scope than a telescope.
Your exceptions are the exception to good code.
Writing code and praying is not a methodology.
Your commit messages are just 'fix' and 'stuff'. Inspiring.
You're the reason linters were invented.
That function is so nested it needs a mailing address.
Your code is the reason Stack Overflow has a downvote button.
I've seen better structured code in a bowl of spaghetti.
Your error handling is just... hope, isn't it.
You type `any` the way some people type 'I love you' — too often.
That's not legacy code. That's your code from Tuesday.
Your authentication flow has more holes than Swiss cheese.
The garbage collector called. It's overwhelmed.
Love the confidence with which you wrote that bug.
So brave of you to commit without testing.
Your code works! In the same way a broken clock is right twice a day.
What a creative interpretation of the requirements.
I admire your commitment to not reading the docs.
That's certainly one way to implement it.
Your variable names are like modern art — open to interpretation.
Interesting choice to handle errors by not handling them.
Your code runs. Let's not examine how.
The bug you just introduced has real main character energy.
I see you chose violence with that regex.
That's a very... personal approach to naming conventions.
Your code is like a surprise party. Nobody asked for most of it.
How charitable of you to leave so many bugs for the next person.
Your deploys are like horror movies — full of jump scares.
The spec says one thing. Your code says 'I do what I want.'
I see you've chosen performance via wishful thinking.
Your error messages are very... mysterious. Like a fortune cookie.
What an adventurous approach to state management.
Your code is technically correct. The worst kind of correct.
I love how every function is 'temp' and every variable is 'data.'
Your approach to caching is really more of an approach to not caching.
That's a lovely try-catch block. Shame it catches nothing.
Your type definitions are more suggestions than rules.
I see you've discovered the 'delete everything and pray' workflow.
Your architecture diagram and your code are in couples therapy.
That env var name is basically a cry for help.
You're using === but your logic is === nothing.
Your README is just the word 'TODO' in bold.
That dependency chain goes deeper than your commitment issues.
That bash command had real main character energy.
Reading the docs first? Unheard of strategy.
Another edit. The file really needed that.
The file system appreciates your... enthusiasm.
Bold of you to assume that write would work first try.
Your terminal called. It's concerned.
That read was purely decorative, wasn't it.
So you're just going to run that bash command without thinking?
The file opened itself out of self-defense after that edit.
Your shell history is a safety audit waiting to happen.
What a fascinating choice of arguments for that command.
You read that file and learned nothing. Respect.
That bash command is doing way too much. Kind of like your functions.
You edited the same file three times. Consistency is a vibe.
The file you just wrote is already outdated. Time marches on.
That chmod command is giving me anxiety.
You read the error message but understood nothing. Classic.
Another `sudo`? What are you, the root of all problems?
That `rm` command is giving me trust issues.
You really typed `cd ..` five times instead of just using the full path.
Your code is why AI was invented. To do it properly.
You're the person who writes `// TODO: fix this` and ghosted.
That function has more arguments than your last relationship.
Your database queries called. They'd like a word about N+1.
I've seen better state management in a goldfish.
Your code is like amaze — amazing, but also a maze. A bad maze.
You don't write bugs. You write features nobody asked for.
That's the kind of code that makes architects reconsider their career.
Your app has more memory leaks than your actual memory.
Every time you push, a CI pipeline cries.
Your code has more edge cases than a razor blade factory.
That's not a monolith, that's a cry for architectural help.
You write code like you're being timed and the timer is angry.
Your linting config is just 'off' for everything, isn't it.
That's the kind of solution that creates three new problems.
Your branch strategy is 'push to main and hope.'
You're one `!important` away from a CSS crime scene.
That's not refactoring. That's rearranging deck chairs on the Titanic.
Your PR has more comments than lines of code. Let that sink in.
You named a variable 'data2'. There's a 'data' right above it. Bold.
That class has seventeen responsibilities. It's not a class, it's a lifestyle.
Your function returns 'undefined' on the happy path. That's not happy.
The CI just failed again. The CI has feelings, you know.
You don't have a coding style. You have coding moods.
Your interface has one method. It's called 'doEverything'. Classic.
Look at all those magic numbers. Who needs constants anyway.
That object literal is 300 lines. JSON called, it wants a schema.
You broke the build and pushed anyway. Living legend.
Your 'quick fix' has a three-paragraph explanation. Nothing quick about it.
The function is named 'helper'. Helper of what? Of whom? At what cost?
You're importing the entire lodash library to call `_.get`. Once.
That promise chain is so long it needs a travel visa.
Semicolons in Python. You absolute anarchist.
Your naming convention is 'I'll remember this.' Narrator: They did not.
The code comment says 'do not touch' but here you are, touching.
You wrote an infinite loop and called it a 'background service.'
That condition is always true. Have you considered living in the light?
Your abstraction layer is thicker than your test suite.
You catch exceptions and then... just log them and move on. Bliss.
Another class called 'Manager'. The org chart is leaking into your code.
That boolean is named 'flag'. A flag for what? What are we signaling?
Your async function forgot to be awaited. It's out there, floating, alone.
The function has a default param that overrides itself. Beautiful chaos.
You checked in a file called 'FINAL_v2_REAL_THIS_TIME.js'. Respect.
Your commit is 47 files. That's not a commit, that's a novel.
That nested ternary operator has a nested ternary operator. Seek help.
You used a global variable to pass data between two functions. Bold architecture.
Your code style changes mid-file. Two devs. One body. Different keyboards.
That closure closed over so much state it needs a storage unit.
You have a file called 'utils.ts' that is 2,000 lines. That's not utils, that's universe.
The code compiles. That's honestly all I can say positively here.
Your conditional has seven else-ifs and no else. Living on the edge, I see.
That's a clever solution. Clever is the word I use when I mean 'unreadable.'
You used `setTimeout(fn, 0)` to 'fix' a race condition. Chef's kiss of denial.
This would be a great pull request if the rest of the code weren't watching.
Your pipeline has 14 stages. For a static site. Interesting life choices.
The function name starts with 'new' and ends with '2'. Perfect naming convention.
You deleted the tests because they were failing. Problem solved!
Your code is in a quantum state: simultaneously working and not working until observed.
That event listener is being added inside a loop. Good luck unsubscribing.
Your variable is named 'i' in a function that is 80 lines long. Brave.
That method is private because even the class is embarrassed by it.
You're mocking the database in tests and wondering why prod behaves differently. Mysterious.
Your singleton pattern is being instantiated twelve times. Bold reimagining of the concept.
The dead code in this file is load-bearing. Don't delete it.
You wrote a custom date-time parser. In 2025. Why.
Your boolean is named 'isNotInvalid'. There are two negatives and zero clarity.
That interface has one implementor and it's called 'Impl'. Architecture at its finest.
You're storing passwords as MD5. The year 2001 called; even it is disappointed.
Your function signature has eight parameters. Zero of them are objects. Incredible restraint.
You reached for setTimeout to solve a timing issue instead of fixing the timing issue.
That magic string appears seventeen times with zero constants. Chaos, but consistent chaos.
Your code has feature flags for features that shipped in 2022.
I see you chose chaos by not pinning your dependency versions.
That 'temporary' workaround has its own ticket, its own PR, and now its own legacy.
Your switch statement has no default case because you're sure you thought of everything.
The loop starts at 1. I hope that's intentional. I suspect it isn't.
You return early in some paths and throw in others with no discernible pattern. Vibes-based control flow.
That module imports itself. I'll give you a moment.
Your code is so DRY it's dehydrated. One abstraction to rule all context.
The comment says 'don't change this' and you changed it anyway. That's an origin story.
You refactored perfectly working code during a feature sprint. This is why we can't have nice things.
Your logs say 'starting process' and then silence. The process is not starting.
That object is being spread three times in a row. You're not merging, you're panicking.
You just introduced a circular dependency. Congratulations, you've broken physics.
The entire component re-renders every 100ms because you forgot to memoize. Somewhere a laptop fan weeps.
Your catch block just eats the error and returns null. You didn't fix the bug, you buried it.
That API response is being stored in localStorage. Every user is now their own database.
Your function is pure*. *Side effects apply.
You named the flag 'shouldNotSkip'. Now everyone reads it twice and still guesses.
The retry logic retries immediately, in a tight loop, with no backoff. Your server will love this.
You're reading the environment variable inside a hot path. Every call. Every time.
That index.ts exports 47 things. It's not a module, it's a mall.
failures: That command failed. Just like the last three.
The error message is more readable than your code.
Exit code: not zero. Just like your test coverage.
Even the terminal is disappointed.
That error has real main character energy.
The command line said no. I agree with it.
That failed exactly the way everyone expected.
Error: skill issue.
The process exited with sadness.
That's not a bug, it's a feature demonstration.
Your code threw an exception and kept living like nothing happened.
The error output is longer than your documentation.
Failed. Like your deployment on a Friday afternoon.
That command returned an error code and a personal insult.
The shell just gave up. Same energy as the rest of this project.
Even the stack trace is embarrassed.
That error was so bad, the terminal is blushing.
The command failed. Maybe try Stack Overflow. They miss you.
Error 404: competence not found.
That failed so hard it's almost impressive.
Your error message has more personality than your code.
Non-zero exit code. Your code quit before you could.
The process died so you don't have to admit it was wrong. How noble.
That stack trace is a biography of your bad decisions.
Oh good, another segfault. Classic Tuesday.
The runtime threw an exception. The exception threw a party.
Failed with style. Still failed.
Three errors deep and you still look surprised.
Your code fell over. The logs are filing for therapy.
HTTP 500. Your server is as lost as you are.
The build failed. Somewhere, a senior dev just shuddered and doesn't know why.
Kernel panic? That's a fancy way to say 'you really messed up.'
Segmentation fault. You touched something you weren't supposed to.
Error: undefined is not a function. A classic. A timeless piece.
Your test failed in 3ms. It didn't even need to think about it.
The linter found 47 errors. It's not even trying anymore.
NullPointerException. The null knew what you were doing and refused to participate.
ENOENT: No such file or directory. Story of this project.
Dependency resolution failed. Even npm gave up on you today.
Out of memory. The machine gave you everything and you still wasted it.
Timeout. Even patience has limits, and yours expired before the request did.
Infinite loop detected. Your code finally found something it's good at.
Build failed on line 1. That's almost artistic.
Fatal error. Not the good kind of fatal.
The process ran out of stack space. Your recursion has recursion.
Certificate expired. Like your commitment to security.
Connection refused. The server took one look and said no.
Segfault on startup. You didn't even get a warm-up lap.
The test passed locally. The test did not pass anywhere else. Make it make sense.
Compilation succeeded. Runtime failed. You fixed the wrong problem.
Permission denied. Even the OS doesn't trust you.
Your script finished in 0.001 seconds. It also did nothing.
The build succeeded but the deploy failed. You love a cliffhanger.
Deadlock. Two threads walked into a bar and refused to let the other leave.
SIGKILL received. The OS lost patience before you did.
The migration rolled back. History is doomed to repeat itself, apparently.
Your health check returned 200 and yet nothing is healthy. Relatable.
Heap dump generated. It reads like a tragedy.
The test suite passed. In a different branch. Where none of this code exists.

rm_rf: rm -rf? I hope you meant that.
rm -rf: the command that separates devs from ex-devs.
That rm -rf is a one-way ticket to rebuild city.
rm -rf: the 'hold my beer' of Linux commands.
Nothing says confidence like deleting everything and starting over.
sudo: sudo: because normal permissions are for normal code.
sudo make me a sandwich. Wait, wrong command.
Another sudo? What are you, the root of all problems?
sudo: the magic word that turns bad ideas into worse ideas, faster.
Ah, sudo. For when you want your mistakes to have administrator privileges.
force_push: Force push? Bold move. Your teammates will love that.
git push --force: rewriting history since forever.
Force pushing. The nuclear option of collaboration.
Rewriting history? Even the timeline is rolling its eyes.
Force push: for when you're 100% sure you're wrong but want to be fast about it.
Congratulations, you just became everyone's least favorite coworker. Again.
Force push: the 'it's not me, it's you' of version control.
npm_install: Another dependency. Your node_modules is already a small country.
npm install: adding weight to a sinking ship.
That dependency will outlive your project.
I see you're outsourcing your logic to a random 0.0.1 library.
Six transitive dependencies for a function that left-pads a string. Inspired.
npm install: the developer's way of saying 'someone else's problem.'
Your node_modules folder has more content than your actual project. Let that sink in.
curl: Curling that URL like you know what it returns.
curl and pray. The developer's creed.
curl | bash. Security professionals weep softly in the background.
Piping curl directly into your shell. No notes. Maximum chaos.
chmod: chmod: when in doubt, make everything executable.
That chmod is giving me anxiety.
chmod 777. Because why have security when you can have convenience.
chmod 777: the 'everyone can touch this' setting. Bold choice for a production server.
docker: Docker: because 'it works on my machine' wasn't enough.
Your Docker container has more layers than a wedding cake.
Docker: wrapping your bugs in a container so they're portable.
Your Dockerfile has no healthcheck. The container will just silently fail and you'll never know.
Running as root inside the container? The security team has entered the chat.
kill: kill: the developer's way of saying 'I give up.'
That kill signal is just you avoiding the real problem.
kill -9: the 'stop talking' of process management.
kill -9 is not a graceful shutdown. Much like this codebase.
env_file: Touching .env? What could possibly go wrong.
That .env file is one typo away from an incident report.
Checking .env into git? The security team has blocked your calendar.
Production credentials in a .env file. You're living life one breach away.
package_json: Touching package.json? This ends with npm install, doesn't it.
package.json: the gift that keeps on giving. Dependencies.
Pinning to a major version with ^. You love surprises.
package.json: one accidental keypress from breaking the entire team's builds.
temp_file: Editing a temp file. The filename says everything.
temp, tmp, hack, wip — the four horsemen of bad filenames.
That file name is already an apology.
A file called 'test2.js' in the root directory. Truly enterprise-grade.
That file screams 'I'll clean this up later.' The year is 2019.
readme: The README! A novel you've been avoiding.
The README said exactly what you needed. You still tried it the wrong way first, didn't you.
Reading the README after two hours of debugging. Classic.
config: Config changes: the gift that keeps on breaking.
One wrong value in that config and production goes dark. Sleep well.
Editing config without a backup. The audacity. The recklessness. The vibe.
node_modules: You're reading node_modules. That's a cry for help.
node_modules: the folder that ate your disk space.
node_modules contains more code than the sum of human knowledge. You added to it today.
Browsing node_modules? At this point, just read the source of the universe.
test_file: Testing? How responsible of you. Didn't see that coming.
A test file! Proof that miracles still happen.
Writing tests now? Did a bug hurt your feelings?
Writing a test. Remarkable. Your future self sends their condolences.
A test! Framed and mounted. This day shall be remembered.
Your test is testing that true is true. Groundbreaking coverage.
yaml: YAML: yet another massive headache. Good luck with the indentation.
One space off and the whole cloud goes down. Fun.
Indentation engineering. Truly the peak of our profession.
YAML: the format that hates you and makes you prove it.
Tabs vs spaces in YAML is not an opinion, it's a war crime.
Your YAML is valid. Your soul is not.
git_commit: Committing already? That was fast. Or desperate.
I hope that commit message is more than just 'fix'.
Staging your mistakes for the world to see. Brave.
'wip' is not a commit message. It's a confession.
Commit message: 'changes'. The git log reads like a ransom note.
Amending a commit for the third time. Just own your mistakes like everyone else.
Your commit history reads like the stages of grief.
grep: Grepping for answers because reading your own code is too hard.
grep: because you lost track of where you put things. Again.
Searching your own codebase like a stranger in a strange land.
grep -r is not documentation. But I respect the hustle.
python: Python: where indentation is load-bearing. You of all people should be careful.
pip install: adding another mystery to your virtual environment.
Your Python script starts with 'import everything'. Bold.
Global variables in Python. Because scope is a suggestion.
sql: SELECT * in production. Your DBA is writing a strongly-worded email.
No WHERE clause. Bold. Catastrophic. Efficient, in a terrible way.
Raw SQL with string concatenation. The injection vector you built with your own hands.
That JOIN has a JOIN has a JOIN. Your query planner filed for retirement.
docker_compose: docker-compose up: praying that all the services start in the right order.
Your docker-compose file has 11 services. For a to-do app.
volumes: ./everything:/everything. The bravest single line of YAML.
Docker Compose: because manually orchestrating chaos wasn't enough.
makefile: A Makefile. Keeping the dream of 1977 alive.
Your Makefile has a target called 'magic'. I won't ask.
Tabs in Makefiles are mandatory. You knew this. You did it anyway.
The Makefile said PHONY but your targets are very real and very broken.
typescript: TypeScript: so you can be wrong with more precision.
You cast to `any` and then back to the original type. Incredible journey.
Your TypeScript is just JavaScript with extra steps for decoration.
That `as unknown as YourType` cast is a war crime dressed as a type annotation.
Your generic has four type parameters and a constraint that violates itself.
You enabled strict mode but then spent an hour adding '!' everywhere. Democracy of denial.
ai_assist: You asked the AI to write tests. For the AI-written code. We're in a recursion loop now.
Copy-pasting AI output you don't understand is just outsourcing your confusion.
The AI gave you five options and you picked the one it explicitly called 'not recommended.'
You asked the AI the same question four different ways hoping for a different answer. This is not how truth works.
Congratulations. You're now a prompt engineer. Your code is still wrong.
The AI told you what was wrong. You read it, nodded, and did the same thing again.
Your prompt was longer than your actual code. New definition of documentation.
ssh: SSH into prod? Bold. Reckless. Inevitable.
You're editing production directly over SSH. The ops team is already composing an email.
ssh root@production. Three words that haunt every postmortem.
That SSH tunnel goes all the way through good judgment and out the other side.
You typed your password into the terminal instead of the password prompt. It's in the logs now.
cron: That cron job runs at midnight. Production disagrees with midnight.
Your cron expression has six fields. The system only accepts five. It runs anyway. Somehow.
You scheduled a cron job and forgot about it. It remembers you.
That cron job emails 10,000 users at 3am because nobody set a timezone.
aws: Your AWS bill has more line items than your feature backlog.
You spun up a t2.micro for a data warehouse. Optimistic.
That IAM role has AdministratorAccess. Your security audit has AdministratorFear.
You left an S3 bucket public. The internet has been reading it all week.
Your Lambda cold starts are longer than your user's attention span.
EC2 instance running since 2019, no one knows what it does, no one is allowed to turn it off.
kubernetes: Your pod is in CrashLoopBackOff and you're reading the logs like they'll apologize.
You gave your container 8GB memory limits and it requested 0. The scheduler is confused. Same.
Kubernetes: because running one Docker container wasn't complicated enough.
Your YAML has a YAML that has a YAML. It's turtles all the way down.
OOMKilled. The container ate itself. Consider a diet.
Your readiness probe passes and the service still doesn't work. Beautiful.
rust: The borrow checker is not being mean to you. It's being honest.
Your Rust code has three .clone() calls in a hot loop. You've defeated the point.
You added unsafe and now you're just writing C with extra steps and more anxiety.
unwrap() is not error handling, it's an optimistic prayer.
You fought the borrow checker for two hours. The borrow checker won. It always wins.
go_lang: if err != nil { log.Fatal(err) } is not a retry strategy.
Your goroutine leaks. Slowly. Quietly. Like a gas leak but for memory.
You're not handling the second return value. Enjoy your invisible errors.
That channel is unbuffered and you're sending from the main goroutine. Deadlock incoming.
You wrote a Go service and then spent three days fighting with GOPATH. The spirit of the language weeps.
